topic: Sharp partial identification of the average treatment effect under bounded collider bias: a closed-form quadratic identified set for the ATE when an analyst conditions (intentionally or by selection) on a partially-determined post-treatment outcome / mediator / selection indicator, characterized by two extremal joint laws (one saturating the upper envelope, one saturating the lower envelope) of the unobserved structural-error pair, with a non-trivial quadratic-in-the-collider-bias-parameter envelope strictly tighter than the Manski no-assumption bounds whenever any bias bound is finite; extends Greenland (2003) and VanderWeele (2009) collider-bias outer bounds to the sharp identified-set characterization, the direct open direction explicitly left in both papers' discussion sections

open_problems.open_problem: Derive the sharp identified ATE interval under a finite bound on collider-induced latent-error dependence after conditioning on a post-treatment variable.

source_refs.claim: Quantifies collider-stratification bias under simple causal models, but treats it as magnitude assessment rather than a sharp ATE identified set.
source_refs.author: Sander Greenland

source_refs.claim: Shows response-type programs can yield sharp closed-form ATE bounds in binary causal systems, but not for post-treatment collider conditioning with curved dependence restrictions.
source_refs.author: Alexander Balke and Judea Pearl
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.excerpt: generic Manski/Balke-Pearl finite-LP argument plus a negative refutation
State an explicit endpoint-table construction target for the mean-kernel polytope

open_problems.why_unsolved: Greenland-style collider algebra and Balke-Pearl-style finite programs identify the right mechanisms but do not give an observed-law-complete characterization for a finite collider-bias parameter. Prior CausalSmith reviews flagged the same gap: generic LP sharpness is not enough unless the endpoint laws are explicit and tied to the causal mechanism.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving closed-form lower and upper ATE envelopes, plus two constructive joint laws of the latent structural-error pair that attain the endpoints and prove sharpness.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize an observable sharp frontier for bad-control or collider conditioning without assuming an unobserved bridge or conditioning law as primitive.

source_refs.claim: Frames selection bias structurally as conditioning on a common effect of variables related to exposure and outcome, but does not solve the latent-error optimization problem.
source_refs.author: Miguel A. Hernan, Sonia Hernandez-Diaz, and James M. Robins

source_refs.claim: Develops monotonic-effect and signed-edge DAG tools for qualitative bias conclusions, leaving exact numerical optimization over compatible latent laws outside the result.
source_refs.author: Tyler J. VanderWeele and James M. Robins

source_refs.excerpt: derive the untreated-history bridge / bridge-CPT from primitive published structural conditions
State the finite-support quadratic program for sup |Omega_bc| with variables

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published DAG literature identifies the collider mechanism and qualitative sign restrictions, while the prior bad-control frontier failed because the bridge/CPT object was treated as freely available. The missing step is an observable restriction set whose feasibility and extrema are derived from primitive laws, not asserted.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-support quadratic program or closed-form theorem with necessary and sufficient observable conditions, explicit endpoint laws, and zero/sign/strict-bias regimes.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove exactly when a finite collider-bias bound strictly tightens the Manski no-assumption ATE interval, including boundary cases where tightening vanishes.

source_refs.claim: Gives simple selection-bias bounding factors, including additive-scale supplements, but these are robustness outer bounds rather than sharp feasible-set characterizations.
source_refs.author: Louisa H. Smith and Tyler J. VanderWeele

source_refs.claim: Estimates ATE and ATT bounds under scalar relaxations of unconfoundedness that approach no-assumption bounds as the sensitivity value grows.
source_refs.author: Matthew A. Masten, Alexandre Poirier, and Linqi Zhang

source_refs.excerpt: proxy-null bound is strictly tighter than the no-proxy endpoint
a rank-one proxy pair can fail point identification and still strictly shorten

open_problems.why_unsolved: Bounding-factor and scalar-sensitivity papers show how restrictions can interpolate toward Manski, but they do not isolate the collider-conditioned endpoint faces that are excluded at each finite bias value. The prior proxy angle states the analogous strict-shortening phenomenon but lacks a robust observable certificate.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A closed-form strict-tightening certificate that identifies the excluded Manski endpoint laws, quantifies interval-width reduction as a function of the collider-bias parameter, and lists equality cases.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop sharp ATE bounds for treatment-induced selection when Lee-style monotone selection is replaced by a finite collider-bias or latent-dependence bound.

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp trimming bounds for treatment effects when outcomes are observed only after nonrandom sample selection affected by treatment, relying on monotonicity of selection.
source_refs.author: David S. Lee

source_refs.excerpt: cap-envelope separation theorem for sharp budgeted conditional trimming
external global-plus-cell manipulation budgets and retained-W equality

open_problems.why_unsolved: Lee sharpness is driven by monotone selection and tail trimming. A finite collider-bias restriction allows both selection-helped and selection-harmed latent types, while the successful RD manipulation bank entry shows that global-plus-cell selection budgets can create sharp envelopes in an adjacent design.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A trimming-free sharp ATE interval that nests Manski at infinite bias, specializes to Lee-type limits under monotone selection, and constructs least-favorable selected laws under finite collider budgets.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend bounded-collider ATE sharp sets from binary post-treatment variables to ordinal or partially determined mediator and selection indicators with global compatibility constraints.

source_refs.claim: Introduces principal stratification for causal effects in the presence of post-treatment variables, but shifts the estimand rather than sharply bounding the full-population ATE after realized conditioning.
source_refs.author: Constantine E. Frangakis and Donald B. Rubin

source_refs.claim: Derives tight bounds for average controlled direct effects with unmeasured intermediate-outcome confounding, not for total ATE distortion induced by conditioning on the intermediate.
source_refs.author: Zhihong Cai, Manabu Kuroki, Judea Pearl, and Jin Tian

source_refs.excerpt: post-treatment intermediate variable is ordinal with K>=3 levels
sharp finite-support adjacent-chain posterior LP with a nonrectangular ordinal compatibility gap

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing post-treatment-variable frameworks either redefine the target as a principal-stratum or controlled-direct-effect estimand. The accepted ordinal principal-strata bank entry solves a related posterior-chain LP, but not the full-population ATE after conditioning on a partially determined mediator or selector.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp identified-set theorem for multi-level or partially determined collider states, with compatibility constraints across states and a reduction to the binary quadratic envelope when K equals 2.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize nonrectangular sharp identified sets when a post-treatment collider and latent proxy or mediator constraints share hidden coordinates.

source_refs.excerpt: two sharp coordinate bounds can be individually valid but cannot generally be combined into a rectangle
shared proxy equations and an unobserved mediator coupling make rectangularization strictly nonsharp

open_problems.why_unsolved: Coordinatewise endpoint sharpness does not imply joint attainability when the same latent coordinates determine both the collider and the outcome-side proxy restrictions. The prior proposals flagged this compatibility failure but did not produce a general collider-conditioned ATE set.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A joint identified-set theorem proving when the scalar ATE envelope is nonrectangular in the underlying endpoint coordinates and constructing compatible extremal laws instead of pasting marginal endpoint solutions.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend linear M-bias sensitivity formulae to a nonparametric sharp ATE identified set under bounded collider-induced latent dependence.

source_refs.claim: Derives formulae for when adjustment for an M-structure inflates or reduces bias, mainly in linear structural equation models.
source_refs.author: Peng Ding and Luke W. Miratrix

open_problems.why_unsolved: Linear structural-equation bias calculations compare adjusted and unadjusted coefficients under parametric assumptions; they do not characterize the full set of bounded-outcome counterfactual laws compatible with an observed conditioned distribution and a finite collider-dependence bound.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A nonparametric quadratic ATE envelope in the collider-bias parameter that reduces to the linear formula in the Gaussian-linear limit and is sharp over bounded-outcome laws.

open_problems.open_problem: Give sharp partial-identification bounds for ATE, direct-effect, or indirect-effect contrasts when both a mediator and an outcome-selection indicator are post-treatment colliders.

source_refs.claim: Identifies direct and indirect effects under sample selection or attrition assumptions and leaves combined sample-selection and mediator-endogeneity extensions as future research.
source_refs.author: Martin Huber

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing mediation-with-selection identification results typically use MAR, IV, control-function, or homogeneity assumptions. Bounded collider bias deliberately weakens those assumptions, so the unresolved object is the joint feasible set over mediator and selection latent errors.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A multivariate extension of the quadratic identified set with separate or joint bounds on mediator and selection collider-bias parameters and endpoint laws attaining each contrast bound.

literature_map:
1. Greenland (2003, Epidemiology) is the central collider-stratification anchor: it quantifies possible bias magnitudes but not a sharp ATE identified set.
2. Hernan, Hernandez-Diaz, and Robins (2004, Epidemiology) give the structural selection-bias DAG in which conditioning on a common effect is the shared mechanism.
3. VanderWeele and Robins (2009, JMLR) provide signed-edge and monotonic-effect tools for qualitative bias conclusions, not sharp numeric intervals.
4. Smith and VanderWeele (2019, Epidemiology) give practical selection-bias bounding factors, including additive supplements, but as outer robustness bounds.
5. Manski (1990) is the no-assumption benchmark that finite collider-bias information should strictly tighten.
6. Balke and Pearl (1997, JASA) show finite response-type programs can yield sharp closed-form treatment-effect bounds in binary systems.
7. Lee (2009, Review of Economic Studies) gives sharp sample-selection trimming bounds under monotone selection; finite collider dependence is a different restriction.
8. Frangakis and Rubin (2002, Biometrics) define principal stratification for post-treatment variables but usually change the estimand rather than bound the marginal ATE after conditioning.
9. Cai, Kuroki, Pearl, and Tian (2008, Biometrics) bound direct effects with confounded intermediates, adjacent to but distinct from total ATE distortion by conditioning.
10. Ding and Miratrix (2015, Journal of Causal Inference) analyze M-bias in linear structural models; the nonparametric sharp-bound analogue remains open.
11. Masten, Poirier, and Zhang (2024, JBES) provide scalar sensitivity-indexed ATE/ATT bounds for relaxed unconfoundedness; the analogous collider-conditioning sensitivity curve is missing.
12. Huber (2020, Econometrics) treats mediation with sample selection under identifying assumptions and points to harder combined mediator-endogeneity and selection extensions.
13. The named tension is between qualitative or outer-bound collider-bias tools and econometric sharp partial-identification objects.
14. The common missing theorem is a finite-parameter ATE set attained by explicit extremal latent-error laws and reducing to Manski only as the bias bound becomes infinite.
prior_proposal_map:
No direct pid_collider_bias_sharp proposal exists under doc/formalization beyond the v1 init state and pipeline stub.
q_did_drdid_ipwdid_frontier_v1 is the closest bad-control/frontier prior; reviewers asked for a primitive finite-support quadratic program rather than free bridge assumptions.
pid_mediation_proxy_v1 supplies the endpoint-construction, strict-Manski-tightening, and nonrectangular joint-set warnings from failed or rejected angles.
eid_frontdoor_multi_mediator_v1 supplies the shared hidden-coordinate warning for mediator coupling and proxy equations.
pid_ordinal_principal_strata_v1 supplies an accepted sharp LP for ordinal post-treatment intermediates and a nonrectangular ordinal compatibility gap.
pid_rdd_manipulation_bounded_v1 supplies an accepted global-plus-cell selection/trimming envelope template.
The appended study-pipeline gaps were mainly LATE, Manski covariate-level-set, and interval-regression inference questions; they did not survive topic-specific G1/G3 filtering for bounded collider bias.
Across prior reviewers, the repeated requirement is explicit extremal-law realization, not generic LP sharpness.
